title_index: A predictive model of tournament outcomes on the pga tour

extraction.one_line_premise: A predictive model for forecasting PGA Tour tournament outcomes based on player performance metrics.
extraction.executive_summary: This model aims to provide probabilistic forecasts for PGA Tour events, focusing on outcomes such as winning, top 5 finishes, top 20 finishes, and making the cut. Key player characteristics include adjusted scoring averages over different time frames, the number of rounds played, and the standard deviation of scores. The model simulates tournament outcomes by combining predicted and random components of player scores, allowing for the estimation of win probabilities and other outcomes through repeated simulations.

detailed_sections.title: Model Overview
detailed_sections.notes: The model generates probabilistic forecasts by simulating tournament outcomes multiple times, using a predicted component based on observable player characteristics and a random component drawn from a probability distribution.

detailed_sections.title: Data and Estimation
detailed_sections.notes: Utilizes PGA Tour, Web.com Tour, and European Tour data from 2013 onwards. Scores are adjusted for field strength and course difficulty using fixed effects regression.

detailed_sections.title: Predicted Component
detailed_sections.notes: Estimated using OLS regression with variables including 2-Year Adjusted Scoring Average, 2-Month Adjusted Scoring Average, Previous Event Adjusted Scoring Average, and Days Since Last Event.

detailed_sections.title: Random Component
detailed_sections.notes: Drawn from a mean-zero normal distribution with player-specific variances, estimated from residuals of player scores.

detailed_sections.title: Model Evaluation
detailed_sections.notes: Evaluated through mean-squared prediction error and calibration of probabilistic forecasts against actual outcomes.

quantitative_claims.claim: Adjusted scoring average can differ significantly from raw field averages.
quantitative_claims.numbers_or_evidence: In the first round at the 2013 Tour Championship, adjusted scoring average was 70.3 compared to a field average of 69.1, a difference of 1.2 strokes.

quantitative_claims.claim: Model predicts win probabilities that align closely with actual outcomes.
quantitative_claims.numbers_or_evidence: In 2016, the model predicted a win probability between 3%-6% for 149 instances, with an actual win probability of 4%.

methodology_steps: Collect data from PGA Tour, Web.com Tour, and European Tour from 2013 onwards.
Adjust scores for field strength and course difficulty using fixed effects regression.
Estimate the predicted component using OLS regression with key player characteristics.
Draw random components from a mean-zero normal distribution with player-specific variances.
Simulate tournament outcomes multiple times to estimate probabilities for winning, top finishes, and making the cut.
modeling_implications: Consider incorporating machine learning techniques for improved predictive power.
Evaluate the impact of additional player characteristics on scoring predictions.
backtest_or_validation_ideas: Use data from 2016 to validate model predictions against actual outcomes.
Perform cross-validation using different years of data to assess model robustness.
related_hypotheses: Player performance is consistent across tournaments, influenced by course-specific factors.
Recent performance has a stronger predictive power than historical averages.
extraction.caveats_and_limits: The model's predictions are limited by the available data and the inherent variability in player performance. Overfitting is less of a concern due to the nature of the data used for estimation.

title_index: A quick look at differences in field quality on the us and european tours

extraction.one_line_premise: Comparison of field quality between the US and European Tours based on player performance.
extraction.executive_summary: This analysis investigates the relative strength of fields in the PGA Tour compared to the European Tour by examining the performance of players who have competed on both tours. A sample of 36 players was analyzed, with findings indicating that players generally perform better on the European Tour, suggesting weaker competition. On average, players performed 0.95 shots better relative to the field on the European Tour than on the PGA Tour, translating to approximately 4 shots per event.

title_index: An intergenerational approach to ranking pga tour players

extraction.one_line_premise: A method for comparing the performances of golfers across different generations using statistical techniques.
extraction.executive_summary: This article presents a methodology for ranking PGA Tour players from different generations by comparing their performances against a common set of competitors. The approach involves breaking down each player's career into 2-year blocks to account for changes in performance over time and to ensure overlapping competition. The resulting metric, the All-Time Performance Index (ATPI), provides a relative measure of a player's performance in a given year compared to the average player in the year 2000. The methodology relies on fixed-effects regressions and random assignment of 2-year blocks to maintain overlap across players' careers.

detailed_sections.title: Intuition
detailed_sections.notes: The method compares players across generations by linking their performances through common competitors, using a 2-year block system to account for changes in player ability over time.

detailed_sections.title: Statistical Details
detailed_sections.notes: The results are based on fixed-effects regressions, with random assignment of players to odd or even year blocks to ensure overlap in performance comparisons.

detailed_sections.title: Results and Interpretation
detailed_sections.notes: The ATPI values reflect differences in single-round scoring averages in a neutral setting, allowing for comparisons across generations.

quantitative_claims.claim: The ATPI value of 3.8 assigned to Rory McIlroy in 2015 indicates he would beat the average PGA Tour player in the year 2000 by 3.8 strokes in a single round.
quantitative_claims.numbers_or_evidence: ATPI value of 3.8 for Rory McIlroy in 2015.

quantitative_claims.claim: The methodology produces a smoothed 3-year average for each player's performance.
quantitative_claims.numbers_or_evidence: Rory's 2015 ATPI is influenced by his performances in 2014 and 2016.

quantitative_claims.numbers_or_evidence: Data from 1983-2017, dropping the first and last years.

methodology_steps: Break each player's career into 2-year blocks.
Randomly assign players to odd or even year blocks to ensure overlap.
Use fixed-effects regression to estimate player performance.
Calculate ATPI as the average performance value across iterations.
modeling_implications: The model can be used to compare players from different eras under controlled conditions.
Adjustments may be needed to account for technological advancements and course conditions.
backtest_or_validation_ideas: Validate the ATPI values against historical performance data of players within the same era.
Check for biases in ATPI values across different generations.
related_hypotheses: Recent players are better due to advancements in technology and training.
Performance metrics should account for the evolution of the sport over time.
extraction.caveats_and_limits: The methodology assumes that player ability is constant on average within a 2-year period, which may not hold true for all players.

title_index: Analysis of tigers past performances coming off long layoffs

extraction.one_line_premise: Examining Tiger Woods' performance after long layoffs to predict future outcomes.
extraction.executive_summary: This article analyzes Tiger Woods' historical performance in tournaments following extended breaks from competitive golf. It highlights a significant decline in performance post-2010 compared to pre-2010, suggesting that while he performed well in comeback events before 2010, he has generally played below his baseline level after 2010. The analysis uses strokes gained metrics to compare his performance in return events to his historical averages, concluding that predicting his current performance remains uncertain due to the lack of a clear baseline.

detailed_sections.notes: Tiger's performance in return tournaments before 2010 was strong, winning 7 of 15 starts with only one finish outside the top 10. Post-2010, he missed the cut more often than finishing in the top 10.

title_index: Benchmarking the models betting performance through 17 weeks

extraction.executive_summary: The article discusses the performance of a predictive betting model over a 17-week period, focusing on Top 20 bets. The model achieved a total return of 151%, significantly outperforming typical betting outcomes. Simulations using adjusted implied probabilities and model probabilities were conducted to assess the model's performance against expected returns, revealing insights into the variance and potential luck involved in the betting outcomes.

title_index: Brief summary of our 2017 in golf betting

extraction.one_line_premise: Summary of Data Golf's betting model performance in 2017.
extraction.executive_summary: In 2017, Data Golf placed 268 bets over 30 weeks, primarily on Top20 finishes. The model's performance showed that realized profit converged to expected profit as the number of bets increased, suggesting effective modeling despite the inherent randomness of golf. The model explained only 6-8% of daily score variation on the PGA TOUR, favored lower-ranked golfers, and emphasized long-term performance over short-term trends. It did not incorporate player-course specific characteristics, which contrasts with market behavior. Overall, the model successfully simulated scores for the entire field, aiding in probability assessments for finishes.

detailed_sections.notes: The model valued 2-year performance heavily, leading to repeated bets on underperforming players.

title_index: Course history revisited course length as an indicator of course fit

extraction.one_line_premise: Evaluating course length as an indicator of course fit for predicting golfer performance.
extraction.executive_summary: This article revisits the predictive power of course history by examining the relationship between golfers' past performances at courses of varying lengths and their future performances. The analysis indicates that while there is a slight correlation between historical performance at long courses and future performance, the overall predictive power remains very low, with R-squared values peaking at 0.2%. The findings suggest that course length may not be a strong predictor of performance, particularly for shorter courses, where the analysis yielded no robust relationships. The article emphasizes the need for larger sample sizes and more nuanced categorization of courses to improve predictive modeling.

detailed_sections.title: Introduction
detailed_sections.notes: The article reviews the importance of course history in predicting golfer performance, focusing on the concept of 'course fit' based on course length.

detailed_sections.title: Methodology
detailed_sections.notes: Historical averages were calculated from 2010 to the year of interest. The dependent variable is the current week's average score, adjusted for player form. Regression analysis used data from 2015-2017, with samples restricted to players with at least 10 or 40 rounds in their course history.

detailed_sections.title: Findings on Long Courses
detailed_sections.notes: Long courses defined as >7500 yards (longest 15%). Regression coefficient for 10 rounds is -0.02, and for 40 rounds is 0.19. R-squared values are very low, peaking at 0.2%.

detailed_sections.title: Findings on Short Courses
detailed_sections.notes: Short courses defined as <7000 yards (shortest 15%). The analysis yielded a slope very close to 0, indicating no predictive power.

detailed_sections.title: Conclusion
detailed_sections.notes: The analysis suggests that while there may be a slight course fit effect for long courses, the overall predictive power is low. The short course analysis did not yield meaningful results.

extraction.one_line_premise: Creating a distance-adjusted measure of driving accuracy in golf.
extraction.executive_summary: This article discusses the concept of distance-adjusted driving accuracy, which accounts for the relationship between driving distance and accuracy off the tee. The author uses trigonometry to derive an adjusted accuracy metric based on average driving distance and distance from the center of the fairway. The analysis is based on 2015 Shot Link data for par 5 tee shots, revealing that while the adjusted rankings do not drastically differ from unadjusted rankings, there is notable movement among players. The method is deemed intuitive and computationally simple, though the author acknowledges potential limitations in the linearity assumption.

extraction.one_line_premise: Examining the predictive power of course history on player performance in golf.
extraction.executive_summary: This article investigates whether a player's historical performance on specific golf courses can predict their future performance. It highlights the importance of empirical evidence in validating the course history hypothesis, noting that while some players have strong course histories, this does not necessarily correlate with future performance. The analysis reveals that course history has a minimal impact on predictive power, with an R-squared value ranging from 0.02% to 0.2% depending on the sample size. The findings suggest that while course history can provide some insight, it is far less predictive than long-term and short-term player form.

extraction.executive_summary: The article examines the effects of the USGA's ban on long putters, focusing on the performance of three major winners who used them: Keegan Bradley, Webb Simpson, and Adam Scott. extraction.one_line_premise: Exploration of first-tee jitters and their impact on golfer performance.
extraction.executive_summary: This article investigates whether first-tee nerves negatively affect golfers' performance by comparing the average performance of players on hole #1 when starting their round versus when playing it as their 10th hole. Using Shot Link data from the PGA Tour in 2015, it was found that players are, on average, almost a full yard further from the center of the fairway on their opening tee shot compared to when they play the same hole later in the round. This difference diminishes in the second round, suggesting heightened nerves during the first round's opening tee shot. Additionally, a comparison of Tiger Woods' opening tee shots to his other tee shots from 2010-2015 indicates he performs better off the first tee in most rounds, although the sample size for first tee shots is smaller, leading to less precise estimates.

detailed_sections.title: Introduction
detailed_sections.notes: The article begins with a personal anecdote about Tiger Woods and the common occurrence of poor opening tee shots post-2009.

detailed_sections.title: Methodology
detailed_sections.notes: The analysis compares average performance on hole #1 for players starting on that hole versus those starting on hole #10 on the same day.

detailed_sections.title: Results
detailed_sections.notes: In the first round, players are almost a full yard further from the center of the fairway on their opening tee shot compared to their 10th hole. This difference disappears in the second round.

detailed_sections.title: Tiger Woods Analysis
detailed_sections.notes: A comparison of Tiger's opening tee shots to his other tee shots shows he performs better off the first tee in most rounds.

detailed_sections.title: Conclusion
detailed_sections.notes: The findings suggest that first-tee nerves may impact performance, but the differences are not substantial.

quantitative_claims.claim: Players are, on average, almost a full yard further from the center of the fairway on their opening tee shot versus when they play it in the middle of the round.
quantitative_claims.numbers_or_evidence: Average distance from center of fairway on hole #1 for opening tee shot vs. 10th hole.

Calculate average distance from the center of the fairway on tee shots hit on hole #1 for players starting on that hole.
Compare this to the average for players on hole #1 that started on hole #10 that day.
Perform analysis separately for rounds 1 and 2.

title_index: How hard is it to follow up a great round with a good round

extraction.one_line_premise: Exploration of the relationship between consecutive rounds of golf performance.
extraction.executive_summary: This article investigates the common belief that it is difficult for golfers to follow up a great round with another good round. Using PGA Tour data from 2000-2016, the analysis calculates 'personal strokes-gained' for players and examines the conditional distributions of performance based on previous rounds. The findings suggest that good or bad form tends to persist across rounds, contradicting the notion that exceptional rounds are unlikely to be followed by good performance.

title_index: How much deeper has golf become in the last 25 years

extraction.one_line_premise: Exploration of how the depth of professional golf has changed over the last 25 years.
extraction.executive_summary: This article analyzes the depth of professional golf over the past 25 years using three measures: the difference between the cut line and leaders after 36 holes, the number of players with at least one top 10 finish per year, and the variance of scoring averages. The findings indicate that while the leaderboard is more bunched, the trend is weaker than expected. The number of players achieving top 10 finishes has steadily increased, suggesting a deeper field of competitors. Additionally, the variance in scoring averages has decreased, indicating that the talent level on tour is becoming more uniform. Overall, the analysis suggests that it is becoming increasingly difficult to win on the PGA Tour due to the growing competition.

detailed_sections.notes: The number of shots separating the leader and the cut line has decreased slightly, with the cut being on average 1 shot closer to the lead in 2015-16 compared to 1983.

detailed_sections.notes: There has been a steady upward trend in the number of unique players achieving at least one top 10 finish, increasing from 138 in 1984 to 195 in 2010, a 41% increase.

detailed_sections.title: Variance of Scoring Averages
detailed_sections.notes: The variance of scoring averages has decreased significantly, indicating that the talent level on tour is becoming more uniform. The variance is now about half of what it was in the 1980s.

title_index: Is golfs next generation as good as we say they are

extraction.one_line_premise: Analyzing the performance of young golfers on the PGA Tour over time.
extraction.executive_summary: This analysis investigates whether the new generation of golfers is indeed better than previous generations by examining their performance metrics on the PGA Tour. The study reveals that while young players today are achieving higher percentages of wins and top finishes, this trend is influenced by the increasing number of young players on Tour. A 'Performance Ratio' is introduced to adjust for this increase, showing that today's young players perform better than their predecessors, particularly when excluding the influence of dominant players like Tiger Woods.

extraction.executive_summary: This article discusses the challenges of analyzing match play data in golf, particularly due to the lack of available statistics. The article also examines the performance of US vs Europe players in match play and highlights the need for further research into predictive modeling for golf tournaments.

It discusses the distribution of average penalty strokes per round among players, the relationship between penalty strokes and course difficulty, and adjustments made for player ability and course effects. The analysis reveals that the majority of players average between 0.25-0.50 penalty strokes per round, with a weak relationship between penalty strokes and scoring averages by course. Adjustments for course-specific averages and player ability using linear regression provide insights into players' performance regarding penalty strokes, particularly focusing on tee shot penalties.

title_index: Predicting the wgc dell match play championship

extraction.one_line_premise: Probabilistic forecasting for the WGC-Dell Match Play Championship using a modified stroke play model.
extraction.executive_summary: This article discusses the methodology for predicting outcomes in the WGC-Dell Match Play Championship, highlighting adjustments made to a standard stroke play model to accommodate the unique format of match play. The model simulates player scores over a maximum of 7 rounds, allowing for round-to-round performance persistence. Match outcomes are determined by comparing simulated scores, with provisions for halved matches and tiebreakers. The model updates predictions after each round based on actual scores, recognizing the limitations of using stroke totals in match play.

title_index: Pressure in golf can we quantify it

extraction.one_line_premise: Exploration of quantifying pressure effects on golfer performance in tournaments.
extraction.executive_summary: The article investigates whether golfers perform worse under pressure, particularly in the final rounds of tournaments. Using ShotLink data from 2012 to 2016, the analysis compares the 4th round scores of players in the top 3 to those outside this group. Initial findings suggest that players in contention perform better, with an average score difference of -0.27. However, a refined analysis comparing individual players' performances under pressure versus non-pressure situations reveals a slight average performance decline of 0.114 relative to the field when under pressure. This indicates that pressure may negatively impact performance, contrary to initial assumptions.

detailed_sections.notes: A weighted average based on the number of pressure situations resulted in a similar value of 0.114, indicating a slight decline in performance under pressure.

detailed_sections.title: Caveats and Considerations
detailed_sections.notes: The analysis acknowledges potential biases, such as players being in contention due to overall better performance that week.

title_index: Should ryder cup partners that win their match play in the next session

extraction.one_line_premise: Exploration of whether Ryder Cup partners that win their matches should play in the next session.
extraction.executive_summary: The analysis of Ryder Cup data from 1991 to 2014 reveals that losing pairs (13-13-4) perform better than winning pairs (17-22-8) when sent out for the next session. Surprisingly, pairs that halved their previous match performed the best (10-4-2). The findings challenge conventional wisdom about momentum in match play and suggest that statistical noise may play a significant role in observed outcomes. A probability analysis indicates an 18% chance that the observed performance of tied pairs is due to randomness, while a broader analysis shows a 45% chance of observing extreme win-loss records across all pairing groups if matches are coin flips.

title_index: Simple linear regression what the heck are we estimating

extraction.one_line_premise: Exploration of the interpretations of regression coefficients in linear regression analysis.
extraction.executive_summary: The article discusses the interpretation of coefficients in simple linear regression, emphasizing that these coefficients can represent different concepts such as marginal effects or average marginal effects depending on the context. It clarifies that regression is a statistical tool that does not inherently imply causality, and highlights the importance of understanding the underlying structural model when interpreting results.

detailed_sections.title: Understanding Regression Coefficients
detailed_sections.notes: Regression coefficients can be interpreted in various ways, including as marginal effects or average marginal effects, depending on the context and assumptions made.

detailed_sections.title: Causal vs. Statistical Relationships
detailed_sections.notes: Regression analysis is a statistical relationship and does not necessarily indicate a causal relationship. The article stresses that regression is a mechanical exercise that can be applied to any set of variables.

detailed_sections.title: Conditions for Causal Interpretation
detailed_sections.notes: For a regression coefficient to be interpreted as a causal effect, certain conditions must be met: the structural relationship must be linear, the causal effect must be constant across individuals, and the independent variable must be uncorrelated with other variables affecting the dependent variable.

modeling_implications: Understanding the distinction between statistical and causal relationships is crucial for accurate modeling in golf analytics.
Regression coefficients should be interpreted with caution, considering the underlying assumptions and model structure.
extraction.caveats_and_limits: The article emphasizes that regression estimates are consistent for population regression coefficients but may not reflect the true causal relationships if assumptions are violated.

extraction.one_line_premise: Analyzing player performance under pressure in golf tournaments using a strokes-gained approach.
extraction.executive_summary: This article presents a method to quantify player performance in contention during golf tournaments by comparing their scores to the field average. The measure focuses on players' performances when they are in contention (top 5 heading into the final round) versus their normal play, using PGA Tour data from 1983-2016. The methodology involves calculating relative scores, defining pressure zones, and averaging these scores to derive a strokes-gained metric. The findings indicate how much better or worse players perform under pressure compared to their usual standards, with implications for understanding player behavior in high-stakes situations.

detailed_sections.title: Methodology Overview
detailed_sections.notes: The measure is constructed by calculating the difference between a player's score and the field average, termed as the relative score. It uses round 1 and 2 scores to establish a baseline for normal play and round 4 scores to assess performance in pressure zones.

detailed_sections.title: Pressure Zones Defined
detailed_sections.notes: Pressure zones are categorized, with 'Playing Near/With the Lead' (PWL) defined as being in 5th place or better heading into the final round, and 'Chasing the Leaders' (CL) as being in 6th-12th place.

detailed_sections.title: Example Calculation
detailed_sections.notes: For Tiger Woods, the average relative score in rounds 1 and 2 is -2.4186, and in the PWL zone, it is -2.7168, indicating he plays 0.2982 shots better than his usual standard when in contention.

detailed_sections.title: Sample Size Considerations
detailed_sections.notes: The statistic may be uninformative for players with few observations in pressure zones, but the dataset from 1983-2016 provides sufficient data for many players.

title_index: Which is more important putting or ball striking

extraction.one_line_premise: Exploration of the importance of putting versus ball-striking in golf performance.
extraction.executive_summary: This article discusses the relative importance of putting and ball-striking in golf, referencing Mark Broadie's analysis which shows that over a season, 72% of variance in strokes-gained is due to long game, while only 17% is due to putting. However, in specific weeks, putting can be more significant, as evidenced by the variance decomposition from the 2017 WGC-Bridgestone Championship, where putting accounted for 38% of variance compared to 15% for off-the-tee play.

extraction.one_line_premise: The Favourite-Longshot Bias is not a bias.
extraction.executive_summary: The article argues that the so-called favourite-longshot bias observed in betting markets, where bets placed at higher odds yield worse rates of return than those at lower odds, is not a result of irrational behavior or market inefficiency. Instead, it presents a model showing that lower returns at longer odds are expected in any betting market with non-zero margin, and highlights the differences in definitions of the bias between traditional and prediction markets.

detailed_sections.title: Introduction
detailed_sections.notes: The favourite-longshot bias is a well-documented observation in betting markets that has been interpreted as a sign of irrational behavior and market inefficiency.

detailed_sections.title: Intuitive Case for Lower Returns
detailed_sections.notes: At very short odds, bookmakers can only apply so much margin without exceeding 100%. A proportional allocation of margin forces bookmakers to reduce total margin, creating a margin-free price on longshots.

detailed_sections.title: Definitions of the Bias
detailed_sections.notes: The FL bias is defined differently in traditional gambling markets (worse returns at longer odds) compared to prediction markets (midpoint of bid-ask spread overestimates objective outcome probability).

detailed_sections.title: Economic Model of Betting Market
detailed_sections.notes: A model is presented where both bookmakers and bettors behave rationally, leading to worse returns at longer odds due to heterogeneous bettor beliefs.

detailed_sections.title: Analysis of Soccer Markets
detailed_sections.notes: Data from soccer markets at a sharp bookmaker (Pinnacle) is analyzed to document the extent of the FL bias.

detailed_sections.title: Conclusion
detailed_sections.notes: The article concludes that the FL bias, as traditionally defined, is not a market inefficiency.

quantitative_claims.numbers_or_evidence: The average total margin for matches in this data is 0.027.

quantitative_claims.numbers_or_evidence: Empirical evidence shows that average returns decline drastically as odds lengthen.

quantitative_claims.numbers_or_evidence: Under reasonable parameterizations of utility, expected returns decline as odds lengthen.

methodology_steps: Analyze the intuitive case for lower returns at longer odds.
Differentiate definitions of the FL bias in traditional vs prediction markets.
Present an economic model of a betting market with heterogeneous bettors.
Analyze data from soccer markets to document the FL bias.
modeling_implications: Lower returns at longer odds should be expected in any betting market with non-zero margin.
The model suggests that heterogeneous bettor beliefs lead to expected returns declining as odds lengthen.
backtest_or_validation_ideas: Validate the model against historical betting data from various sports.
Test the model's predictions on different betting markets.
related_hypotheses: The absence of a bias in the midpoint of the bid-ask spread implies returns will decrease as odds lengthen.

extraction.caveats_and_limits: The analysis is based on the assumption that bettors have heterogeneous beliefs and that bookmakers set prices to maximize expected utility.

title_index: How sharp are bookmakers? Part II

extraction.one_line_premise: Analysis of bookmaker sharpness and calibration in golf betting.
extraction.executive_summary: This article examines the performance of various sportsbooks in terms of margin, calibration, and betting results for golf matchups and 3-balls over the 2021 and 2022 seasons. It highlights discrepancies in bookmaker odds, the presence of a favourite-longshot bias, and the calibration of Data Golf's predictions against actual outcomes.

detailed_sections.title: Analyzing books: margin and calibration
detailed_sections.notes: The analysis of margins and returns from various sportsbooks shows that Bet365 had the best return from a blind betting strategy on ties-void matchups, while Pinnacle's blind return worsened significantly from -3.7% in 2019-2020 to -5.6% in 2022. The presence of a favourite-longshot bias was noted, particularly with Circa and Unibet.

detailed_sections.title: Analyzing Data Golf: calibration
detailed_sections.notes: The calibration plot for matchups indicates that favoured golfers win less than predicted, while underdogs win more. The model appears overconfident, particularly in the 55-60% win probability bin.

detailed_sections.title: Analyzing Data Golf: betting results and odds movement

extraction.one_line_premise: Analysis of the importance of specific holes at Augusta National based on variance in scores.
extraction.executive_summary: This article analyzes the contribution of each hole at Augusta National to the total variance in scores during The Masters from 1983 to 2017. Using variance decomposition, it identifies critical holes, particularly in the back nine, while also noting surprises such as the seventh hole's increasing importance post-2000. The findings align with traditional narratives about Amen Corner but also highlight the impact of course changes on scoring dynamics.

detailed_sections.title: Methodology
detailed_sections.notes: Variance decomposition is used to assess the contribution of each hole to the total variance in scores. The analysis excludes the 1985 and 1988 tournaments due to lack of data.

detailed_sections.title: Key Findings
detailed_sections.notes: The thirteenth hole has the highest average contribution to variance at 8.4%. Other significant holes include fifteen, twelve, and sixteen. The seventh hole's importance has increased post-2000.

detailed_sections.title: Trends Over Time
detailed_sections.notes: The fifteenth and second holes exhibit downward trends in their contributions to variance since changes were made around 2000.

detailed_sections.title: Round-Specific Contributions
detailed_sections.notes: The thirteenth hole consistently contributes the most across all rounds, while the seventh hole shows significant contribution in the fourth round.

detailed_sections.title: Top Finishers' Performance
detailed_sections.notes: Analysis of scoring averages for top 10 finishers shows similar trends to variance contributions, with the seventh hole emerging as critical.

quantitative_claims.numbers_or_evidence: Ranking changes observed in scoring averages.

extraction.one_line_premise: Analysis of the hardest around-the-green shots at Augusta National using strokes-gained data.
extraction.executive_summary: This article ranks the five hardest around-the-green spots at Augusta National based on data from the 2021 Masters. The analysis utilizes strokes-gained metrics to estimate shot difficulty relative to the PGA Tour baseline, employing an empirical Bayes method to address sample size issues. Each shot's difficulty is assessed based on its proximity to the pin and the concept of 'short-sidedness'.

detailed_sections.notes: Shots are grouped by pin position, around-the-green quadrant, and lie. Difficulty is estimated using strokes-gained relative to the PGA Tour baseline.

detailed_sections.notes: From 2004 to 2011, the top 25 longest hitters gained an average of 0.25 strokes per round. From 2012 to 2018, this increased to 0.7 strokes per round, showing a difference of 0.45 strokes per round.

detailed_sections.notes: Richy Werenski finished 126th in the FedExCup standings despite averaging -0.01 true strokes gained, which was better than his -0.21 average in 2018.

extraction.one_line_premise: A numerical summary of current happenings in the world of golf, focusing on rankings and performance metrics.
extraction.executive_summary: The article discusses updates to the Data Golf rankings, highlighting discrepancies between the Data Golf rankings and the Official World Golf Rankings (OWGR). It emphasizes the performance of golfers across various tours and the allocation of OWGR points, particularly noting the unfair treatment of the Mackenzie Tour compared to the Asian Tour. The article also touches on the expected OWGR points for average players across different tours.

detailed_sections.title: Golfers in Rankings
detailed_sections.notes: 11 golfers from the Japan Golf Tour are in the top 300 of Data Golf rankings, while 15 are in the OWGR, totaling 26. Ryo Ishikawa is notably ranked 342nd by Data Golf despite being 97th in OWGR.

detailed_sections.title: Tour Representation
detailed_sections.notes: 9 tours are represented in the top 300 of Data Golf rankings, with top players from each tour listed.

detailed_sections.title: OWGR Points Analysis
detailed_sections.notes: Average performance in Mackenzie Tour events is -2.69 true strokes-gained per round, while Asian Tour events average -2.72. Winners of Asian Tour events receive 8.7 more OWGR points than Mackenzie Tour winners.

detailed_sections.title: Expected OWGR Points
detailed_sections.notes: Typical full-field PGA Tour events yield about 1.4 expected OWGR points for average players, while European Tour events yield 1.8, major championships yield 2.3, and World Golf Championships yield 2.7.

detailed_sections.title: Fairness in OWGR Points Allocation
detailed_sections.notes: The article discusses the fairness of OWGR point allocation, noting that the TOUR Championship awards too many points to lower finishing positions compared to other events.

extraction.executive_summary: This article ranks the five hardest around-the-green spots at Augusta National using data from the 2021 Masters. Each green surround is divided into quadrants, and shots are grouped by pin position and lie. The difficulty is estimated using strokes-gained relative to the PGA Tour baseline, with an empirical Bayes method applied to account for sample size issues. The analysis provides insights into shot difficulty and player performance in these challenging areas.

extraction.one_line_premise: Discussion of fundamental changes in the PGA model's methodology for estimating player skill.
extraction.executive_summary: The 2022 offseason saw a refinement in the methodology for estimating a player's baseline skill in the PGA model. This change aims to improve the accuracy of predictions by addressing two key issues: the assumption of equal course difficulty for all players and the need to adjust for course-specific skill influences. The model now incorporates a fitting process to estimate course difficulty based on tee times, and adjusts historical performance data to better reflect a player's skill at neutral courses. These adjustments, while small, are expected to enhance predictive power and account for variations in course fit.

extraction.one_line_premise: Tweaks to the PGA model for improved predictive power in 2024.
extraction.executive_summary: The article discusses several key updates to the PGA model for 2024, focusing on changes in how player performance is weighted based on past rounds, the impact of days since last event (DSLE), and adjustments to skill estimates for strokes-gained categories. The new weighting scheme emphasizes more recent rounds while still considering long-term performance, aiming to better capture player trends. Additionally, the treatment of DSLE now accounts for the relative performance of the field, potentially altering predictions based on how recently players have competed. Lastly, improvements have been made to skill estimates for players with limited data in certain categories, ensuring more accurate projections.

detailed_sections.title: Weighting Past Rounds
detailed_sections.notes: The model now uses a variable decay weighting scheme for past rounds, emphasizing recent performance while still considering long-term form. The new scheme allocates 48% of the weight to the most recent 20 rounds, 8.4% to rounds 1-19, -14.1% to rounds 20-126, and 7.6% to rounds beyond 150.

detailed_sections.title: Days Since Last Event (DSLE)
detailed_sections.notes: The model adjusts the impact of DSLE based on the field's average DSLE, allowing for a more nuanced understanding of how recent play affects performance. If a player competed 1 week ago while the field played 2 weeks ago, this could add 0.1-0.15 strokes to their performance prediction.

detailed_sections.title: Skill Estimates for SG Categories
detailed_sections.notes: Improvements have been made to skill estimates for strokes-gained categories, particularly for players with limited recent data. The model now applies regression to the mean for players with fewer rounds of SG data, ensuring that predictions are more aligned with overall skill.

extraction.one_line_premise: Tweaks to the PGA model for improved predictive power in 2024.
extraction.executive_summary: The article discusses several key updates to the PGA model for 2024, focusing on changes to how player performance is weighted based on past rounds, the impact of days since last event (DSLE), and improvements in skill estimates for strokes-gained categories. The new weighting scheme emphasizes recent performance while still accounting for long-term form, and the treatment of DSLE now considers the relative performance of the field. These changes aim to enhance the model's accuracy and predictive capabilities.

detailed_sections.title: Weighting Past Rounds
detailed_sections.notes: The model now uses a variable decay scheme for weighting past rounds, giving more weight to recent rounds and less to older ones. Specifically, it puts 48% weight on the most recent 20 rounds, 8.4% on rounds 1-19, -14.1% on rounds 20-126, and 7.6% on rounds more than 150 rounds into the past.

detailed_sections.title: Days Since Last Event (DSLE)
detailed_sections.notes: The model now adjusts the effect of DSLE based on the field's average DSLE. If a player competed 1 week ago when the field mostly played two weeks ago, this could be worth 0.1-0.15 strokes. If the field's average DSLE is 21 (3 weeks), the benefit of playing 1 week more recently might only be worth 0.04-0.06 strokes.

extraction.one_line_premise: Updates to the PGA stroke-gained model for 2026 focusing on standard deviations and shot-level data.
extraction.executive_summary: This article discusses two significant changes made to the PGA stroke-gained model during the 2026 offseason. The first change involves a more complex approach to projecting players' standard deviations (SDs), which now accounts for skill level and driving distance. The second change incorporates shot-level data to better reflect the predictive power of individual shots, particularly in terms of diminishing returns for strokes-gained metrics. These adjustments aim to improve the accuracy of player performance predictions in tournament simulations.

extraction.one_line_premise: Analyzing Brent Grant's putting performance and its implications for predictive modeling.
extraction.executive_summary: Brent Grant's putting performance, averaging -2.8 strokes-gained per round over four rounds, raises questions about the accuracy of predictive models in estimating future performance. The model predicts his putting skill to be -0.72 strokes per round, which is significantly below average. This analysis highlights the challenges of using limited data to predict performance and the importance of considering overall skill levels.

extraction.one_line_premise: Analysis of whether the model overvalues leaders in PGA and European Tour events.
extraction.executive_summary: The analysis examines the win rates of players entering the final round with a share of the lead or better, comparing predicted win rates from the model against actual outcomes. The model slightly underestimated the win rates for 54-hole leaders on both the PGA and European Tours, particularly for elite players. The findings suggest a divergence between the model's predictions and betting market expectations, especially for unproven players or those with a history of losing leads. The analysis also highlights the impact of player skill levels on win rates and suggests potential adjustments to the model to account for pressure effects based on skill.

detailed_sections.title: Model Predictions vs. Actual Outcomes
detailed_sections.notes: 339 players entered the final round with a share of the lead or better on the PGA Tour, with predicted wins of 127.8 (37.6% win rate) and actual wins of 134 (39.5%). For the European Tour, 83 players had a lead or co-lead, with predicted wins of 26.6 (32%) and actual wins of 29 (34.9%).

detailed_sections.notes: Grouped by pre-tournament skill, elite players outperformed model expectations, while sub-elite players underperformed. PGA Tour skill classifications: elite (+1.6), sub-elite (0.8 to 1.6), above-average (0 to 0.8), below-average (<0).

extraction.one_line_premise: Exploration of modeling pressure in professional golf and its impact on performance.
extraction.executive_summary: This article revisits the concept of pressure in professional golf, focusing on how to estimate a 'pressure-free' baseline for player performance. The author discusses the challenges of modeling pressure, particularly how a player's leaderboard position influences their performance in final rounds. The analysis highlights the importance of controlling for pressure when estimating the relationship between earlier round performances and final round outcomes. The findings suggest that while pressure affects performance, there are methods to isolate the impact of earlier rounds on final round performance, particularly in specific tournament contexts like the Tour Championship.

extraction.one_line_premise: Exploring the implications of variance in golf scores and its impact on performance measurement.
extraction.executive_summary: This article discusses the variation in golf scores, particularly focusing on the standard deviation of scores in PGA Tour events since 2015. It highlights the importance of standardizing scores to accurately measure performance, especially in rounds with varying levels of difficulty and skill distribution. The analysis reveals that about 25% of the variance in scores can be explained by course characteristics, with the difficulty of the course being the strongest determinant. The article concludes that while standardizing scores may seem beneficial, it risks losing valuable information about performance, particularly in rounds influenced by penalty strokes.

extraction.one_line_premise: Exploring the impact of weighting rounds by sequence versus time in golf performance modeling.
extraction.executive_summary: This article discusses the methodology of estimating baseline skill in golf models by averaging historical performances with decreasing weights based on the recency of rounds played. It contrasts two weighting methods: sequence-based and time-based, highlighting their respective strengths and weaknesses. The article presents a case study of golfer Brendan Jones, illustrating how the choice of weighting can significantly affect skill predictions, especially for players with sparse data. A new approach is proposed that adjusts the weight applied to time-weighted averages based on the variance of estimates, improving prediction accuracy for players with limited recent performances.

detailed_sections.notes: Jones' prediction highlighted the limitations of the model, as his recent performance was not accurately reflected due to sparse data.

detailed_sections.notes: The article proposes using the variance of estimates to adjust the weight given to time-weighted averages, improving prediction accuracy.

detailed_sections.title: Conclusion and Implications
detailed_sections.notes: The new method provides a more parsimonious solution for adjusting weights based on player performance history.

extraction.one_line_premise: Understanding the importance of modeling ties in golf match-up simulations.
extraction.executive_summary: This article discusses the significance of modeling tie probabilities in golf match-ups, even when ties are void. It explains how different simulation methods (with and without ties) yield different expected value estimates and emphasizes that excluding ties can lead to overestimating break-even probabilities. The article provides mathematical expressions for calculating break-even implied probabilities in various scenarios, highlighting the impact of ties on expected value assessments.

detailed_sections.notes: Modeling tie probabilities is crucial for accurate expected value assessments in golf match-ups.

detailed_sections.notes: The break-even implied probability is derived from win probabilities and is affected by the presence of ties.

detailed_sections.notes: Three scenarios are analyzed: 1) with ties void, 2) without ties, and 3) with dead-heat rules.

detailed_sections.title: Conclusion
detailed_sections.notes: Excluding ties can lead to overestimating break-even probabilities, especially in lopsided match-ups.

extraction.one_line_premise: Exploring how golfers perform under pressure when near the lead in tournaments.
extraction.executive_summary: This analysis investigates the performance of golfers in pressure situations, particularly when they are leading or near the lead in tournaments. Using a predictive model based on historical performance, the study compares actual performance against expected performance to assess the impact of pressure. Key findings include that final round leaders perform an average of 0.44 strokes below expectation, indicating a trend of underperformance under pressure. The analysis also introduces metrics such as conditional expected wins to evaluate golfers' performances relative to their win probabilities.

detailed_sections.notes: Competitive golf places players under pressure, affecting their performance. This analysis compares performance in pressure situations to baseline expectations.

detailed_sections.notes: The analysis suggests incorporating starting position into predictive models, as it significantly impacts performance expectations.

extraction.one_line_premise: Three metrics proposed to quantify the entertainment value of PGA Tour tournaments.
extraction.executive_summary: This article introduces three metrics—Excitement, Volatility, and Star Power—to evaluate the entertainment value of golf tournaments. These metrics are based on live win probability data and aim to capture the dynamics of competition, the unpredictability of outcomes, and the presence of top players. The metrics are correlated and provide a comprehensive view of tournament entertainment, although they may not account for all subjective aspects of viewer enjoyment.

detailed_sections.title: Excitement
detailed_sections.notes: Measures uncertainty around who will win. Defined as one minus the sum of win probabilities squared, averaged over time. Higher values indicate more excitement.

detailed_sections.title: Volatility
detailed_sections.notes: Captures turnover in win probabilities. Defined as the sum of absolute changes in player-level win probabilities, ignoring changes smaller than 1%.

detailed_sections.title: Star Power
detailed_sections.notes: Measures the average skill of contending players. Defined as the win-probability-weighted average of player skill, averaged over time.

quantitative_claims.numbers_or_evidence: Top 5: 2021 Rocket Mortgage (0.988), 2019 Honda Classic (0.985), 2021 Travelers Champ. (0.985), 2020 Charles Schwab (0.984), 2022 RBC Heritage (0.984)

quantitative_claims.claim: Volatility metric values for top tournaments.
quantitative_claims.numbers_or_evidence: Top 5: 2022 Arnold Palmer (17.1), 2021 Wyndham Champ. (16.9), 2023 Arnold Palmer (16.8), 2020 Hero Challenge (16.2), 2022 Hero Challenge (15.9)

quantitative_claims.claim: Star Power metric values for top tournaments.
quantitative_claims.numbers_or_evidence: Top 5: 2023 Genesis Invit'l (+1.95), 2023 WM Phoenix Open (+1.85), 2019 WGC-Mexico Champ. (+1.83), 2019 Hero World Challenge (+1.79), 2023 U.S. Open (+1.78)

quantitative_claims.numbers_or_evidence: Correlation of 0.7

definitions_and_terms: Excitement: One minus the sum of win probabilities squared, averaged over time.
Volatility: Sum of the absolute value of changes in player-level win probabilities.
Star Power: Win-probability-weighted average of player skill, averaged over time.
methodology_steps: Collect live win probability data every 5 minutes during tournaments.
Calculate Excitement by averaging the adjusted squared sum of win probabilities.
Calculate Volatility by summing absolute changes in win probabilities, ignoring changes smaller than 1%.
Calculate Star Power using win-probability-weighted averages of player skill.
Combine metrics into overall entertainment scores using percentile form.
modeling_implications: Incorporate live win probability data into tournament simulations.
Adjust metrics to account for field size effects on entertainment value.
Explore the correlation between metrics to refine entertainment scoring.
backtest_or_validation_ideas: Validate metrics against viewer engagement data.

Compare entertainment scores with betting market reactions.
related_hypotheses: Higher Star Power correlates with higher viewer engagement.
Tournaments with high Volatility are perceived as more exciting.
extraction.caveats_and_limits: Metrics may not capture all subjective aspects of entertainment value, such as the prestige of tournaments.

title_index: What makes Justin Thomas a great iron player?

extraction.one_line_premise: Analyzing Justin Thomas' approach game data using strokes-gained metrics.
extraction.executive_summary: Justin Thomas has consistently ranked among the top approach players on the PGA Tour over the last five years. This analysis delves into his performance using strokes-gained metrics, adjusted proximity, adjusted GIR, good shot %, and poor shot avoidance %. By breaking down his approach performance into specific distance bins, we reveal that Thomas excels particularly at shorter approach distances, gaining strokes through a high rate of good shots rather than avoiding poor shots. The stability of these metrics over time is also discussed, highlighting adjusted proximity as the most stable statistic.

extraction.one_line_premise: Analyzing Justin Thomas' approach game data using strokes-gained metrics.
extraction.executive_summary: Justin Thomas has consistently excelled as an iron player on the PGA Tour, particularly in approach shots from under 200 yards. This analysis delves into his performance metrics, including strokes-gained, adjusted proximity, adjusted GIR, good shot %, and poor shot avoidance %. The findings indicate that while Thomas has a high rate of good shots, he struggles with poor shot avoidance, suggesting a high-risk, high-reward strategy that has proven effective in his approach game.

title_index: Where to miss at TPC Sawgrass

extraction.one_line_premise: Analysis of optimal miss strategies at TPC Sawgrass for the 2022 PLAYERS Championship.
extraction.executive_summary: The article examines the penalties associated with missing fairways left versus right at TPC Sawgrass, emphasizing that missing left is significantly more penalizing. The analysis uses shot-level data to inform players about where to miss on the course, suggesting that players should avoid left misses due to the higher stroke penalties. The findings indicate that driving accuracy is crucial for success at this course, and players who can avoid left misses are likely to perform better.

extraction.one_line_premise: Analyzing the impact of fairway width on the performance of different types of golfers.
extraction.executive_summary: This article explores the relationship between fairway width and the performance of golfers categorized by their driving accuracy and distance. It challenges the notion that wider fairways benefit short and accurate golfers while narrow fairways favor long and inaccurate golfers. The analysis reveals that the advantage of accurate golfers in hitting fairways remains relatively constant across varying fairway widths, and that hole characteristics such as length and missed-fairway penalties are more significant predictors of performance than fairway width itself.

detailed_sections.title: Introduction
detailed_sections.notes: The article introduces the debate on whether wider fairways benefit short and accurate golfers compared to narrow fairways that may favor long and inaccurate golfers.

detailed_sections.title: Driving Accuracy Analysis
detailed_sections.notes: The analysis uses PGA Tour data from 2019-2021, focusing on non-par 3 holes to estimate driving accuracy differences between accurate and inaccurate golfers based on fairway width.

detailed_sections.notes: The article summarizes strokes-gained performance of different golfer groups based on driving accuracy percentages, revealing counterintuitive results.

detailed_sections.title: Regression Analysis
detailed_sections.notes: Regression coefficients are presented to quantify the impact of various hole characteristics on golfer performance.

detailed_sections.title: Conclusion
detailed_sections.notes: The article concludes that fairway width does not significantly favor any specific group of golfers and emphasizes the importance of hole length and missed-fairway penalties.

title_index: Who plays well at The Open?

extraction.one_line_premise: Using performance trends by golfer nationality to enhance predictions for The Open Championship.
extraction.executive_summary: The article discusses a methodology for predicting golfer performance at The Open Championship by analyzing performance trends based on golfer nationality. It emphasizes the importance of course fit and history, while introducing a broader approach that incorporates nationality-based adjustments. The analysis reveals that certain nationalities tend to over or underperform at specific events, and these insights can be used to refine predictions, especially when historical data is limited. The methodology includes defining baselines for performance, estimating nationality-country effects, and adjusting predictions based on individual player histories.

detailed_sections.notes: Examines how golfers from different nationalities perform at events in various countries, allowing for adjustments even with limited course data.

detailed_sections.notes: Highlights the importance of individual player histories in refining predictions beyond nationality averages.

detailed_sections.notes: Describes how nationality-specific estimates are combined with individual player data to create a Links adjustment for performance predictions.

extraction.caveats_and_limits: The methodology relies on the availability of sufficient historical data to make meaningful adjustments. Performance in golf is inherently noisy, and small sample sizes can significantly affect predictions.
